coredata.dc:title: Tuning of (E)-(4-fluorophenyl)-1,1-diamino-2,3-diazabuta-1,3-diene nanostructures for the selective detection of imidacloprid
coredata.dc:creator: Bhattu, Monika
Verma, Meenakshi
Wani, Aabid A.
Bharatam, P.V.
Sareen, Shweta
Kathuria, Deepika
coredata.dc:description: The present work demonstrates a facile route for synthesizing the organic nanoparticles (ONPs) and the blue fluorescent Quantum Dots (QDs) based on an organic molecule named (E)-(4-fluorophenyl)-1,1-diamino-2,3-diazabuta-1,3-diene. The synthesis process possesses advantages viz green synthesis, non-toxic degraded products, and amount of organic compound. Initially, the ONPs were prepared using the nanoprecipitation method and were screened for their recognition potential against various pesticides, however, no selectivity has been observed. This motivated us to tune the ONPs into QDs. The QDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method and a color change was observed in the QDs solution under daylight and under a UV lamp. The emission wavelength was observed at 400 nm (λexcitation = 278 nm). The synthesized QDs exhibited selective sensing potential towards imidacloprid via a quenching mechanism. A normalised decrement in the luminescence intensity of QDs was observed on raising the concentration of imidacloprid and a good linear response was noticed over a concentration varies from 1 μM to 100 μM with a regression coefficient of 0.99. The detection limit was estimated to be 4.53 nM and quantification limit was calculated to be and 13.72 nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 1,1-Diamino azine
Imidacloprid
Fluorescence spectroscopy
Sensor
Quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nanostructures (organic nanoparticles and quantum dots)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nanoprecipitation of Organic Nanoparticles of compound 3.
The Overlapping fluorescence spectra of Organic Nanoparticles (ONPs) and Quantum Dots (QDs).
The (a) and b) represents the shape of ONPs and QDs using TEM; c) and d) represents the particle size distribution of ONPs and QDs respectively.
Fluorescence spectra of a) ONPs; b) QDs against different pesticides and metal ions.

a) Titration of QDs with the subsequent addition of imidacloprid (0 μM–100 μM); b) Illustration of normalised decrease with increase in the concentration of imidacloprid; c) Stern Volmer Plot for estimating the sensitivity of synthesized sensor; d) Fluorescence response of the synthesized sensor for imidacloprid in presence of other competing pesticides illustrating no variation in fluorescence response. Herein, IDC stands for imidacloprid.

Fluorescence response of the synthesized sensor for imidacloprid a) in presence of interfereing metal ions; b) pH study varying from 2 to 10; and c) temperature study varying from 15 to 60 °C. Herein, IDC stands for imidacloprid.
Pausible mechanism for the detection of imidacloprid using QDs.
HOMO−LUMO orbitals of complexes and Imidacloprid.
FTIR spectra of a) 3 and b) 3 + Imidacloprid.
Recent approaches for the detection of imidacloprid.
TD-DFT results for Com-a, b, c, d and Imidacloprid.

head.ce:title: Tuning of (E)-(4-fluorophenyl)-1,1-diamino-2,3-diazabuta-1,3-diene nanostructures for the selective detection of imidacloprid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Center for Research and Development, Chandigarh University, Gharuan, Punjab, 140413, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University Center for Research and Development

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University Center for Research and Development, Chandigarh University, Gharuan, Punjab, 140413, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Mohali, Punjab, 160062, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Mohali, Punjab 160062, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work demonstrates a facile route for synthesizing the organic nanoparticles (ONPs) and the blue fluorescent Quantum Dots (QDs) based on an organic molecule named (E)-(4-fluorophenyl)-1,1-diamino-2,3-diazabuta-1,3-diene. The synthesis process possesses advantages viz green synthesis, non-toxic degraded products, and amount of organic compound. Initially, the ONPs were prepared using the nanoprecipitation method and were screened for their recognition potential against various pesticides, however, no selectivity has been observed. This motivated us to tune the ONPs into QDs. The QDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method and a color change was observed in the QDs solution under daylight and under a UV lamp. The emission wavelength was observed at 400 nm (λexcitation = 278 nm). The synthesized QDs exhibited selective sensing potential towards imidacloprid via a quenching mechanism. A normalised decrement in the luminescence intensity of QDs was observed on raising the concentration of imidacloprid and a good linear response was noticed over a concentration varies from 1 μM to 100 μM with a regression coefficient of 0.99. The detection limit was estimated to be 4.53 nM and quantification limit was calculated to be and 13.72 nM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute pesticide toxicity is a serious public health issue around the world, with organophosphorus (OPs) poisoning accounting for the majority of lethality. As a result, alternative pesticides are employed in the hopes of being less dangerous to humans while still being effective at controlling pests. (Wirtschaftswissenschaften, 2008), (Bhattu et al., 2021) Pesticides, such as neonicotinoid chemicals, that were developed as alternatives to poisonous OPs. These are increasingly being utilized due to their less detrimental effects in human. (Jeschke and Nauen, 2008), (Creedon et al., 2020), (Li et al., 2020) Though these alternatives are considered quite safer, their exposure may lead to acute toxicity and life threatening consequences. Neonicotinoid pesticides are the natural nicotine that irreversibly binds with nAChRs present in insects (Nauen and Bretschneider, 2002). The binding may cause the accumulation of the complex in nervous system and leads to chronic effects for non targeted invertebrates. (Yamamuro et al., 2019), (Xiong et al., 2019), (Morrissey et al., 2015) The examples of most commonly used neonicotinoid pesticides are imidacloprid (I), thiamethoxam (II), clothianidin (III), acetamiprid (IV), thiacloprid (V), dinotefuran (VI), and nitenpyram (VII) (Fig. 1 ).

Imidacloprid was identified as an effective alternative to the OPs and is introduced by ingestion or contact (Lamers et al., 2011). The mineralization of the pesticide is incomplete under ambient conditions and will oxidize to metabolites by forming the intermediates having heteroatom(s) (Grover et al., 2014). Imidacloprid and its metabolites such as 6-hydroxynicotinic acid and imidacloprid-urea, 6-chloronicotinic (CNA) are not degraded so ideasily and persist in soil and environment for a long time (NDEZ-BAYO et al., 2009). Imidacloprid binding efficacy with post-synaptic nicotinic acetylcholine receptors offers different kinds of inhibitory mechanism. For instance, in case of insects, it leads to the breakdown of neuron for the propagation of signal, paralysis or death of insect. (Guzsva'ny et al., 2008), (Kim et al., 2003) The toxicity caused by imidacloprid is lesser via dermal exposure (Wismer, 2004), moderate via ingestion and variable via inhalation. In case of dust and aerosols, the aerosols of imidacloprid are highly toxic in comparison to its dust particles. The LC50 of the aerosol form for imdacloprid 0.05 mg/L for inhalation and the moderate oral toxicity varies from 50 to 500 mg/kg.

Taking its toxicity into consideration, it became crucial to detect imidacloprid in water, agriculture products, and soil. Till now, numerous methods have been developed based on the sophisticated instruments such as ELISA, Diode Array Detection, HPLC, (Fernandes et al., 2018), (Liu et al., 2005), (Vilchez et al., 1996) (OBANA et al., 2002), GCMS, (Vilchez et al., 1996) (Naval'ona et al., 1997), and LC-MS(Bhattu et al., 2022). However, the main disadvantage of the above given methods are their analysis procedure (Sample pretreatment, analysis, instrumentation, technical personnel) and their high cost. Apart from them various electrochemical, fluorescence or colorimetric strategies situated on the nanomaterials have also been established which either involves the use of enzymatic assay (Li et al., 2019), (Wen et al., 2017), (Fu et al., 2016) (Table 1 ) which accounts for the less thermal stability and low shelf life. Accounting the limitations of reported methods and to overcome these limitations, in recent years, quantum dots (QDs) based fluorescence recognition studies have been carried out as an alternative to above mentioned techniques.

QDs are basically the crystalline nanostructures, which possess distinct optical, electronic, and sensing characteristics due to quantum confinement phenomenon (Nsibande and Forbes, 2016). Due to these properties, their applications have been widely explored in biological labels (Liu et al., 2011), microelectronics (De Menezes et al., 2005), electrochemistry (Huang and Zhu, 2013) and in solar cells (Beard et al., 2010). The core of QDs is generally synthesized using transition metals which are toxic in nature.

Hence, in the present work, we developed the 1,1-diamino azine derived QDs to detect imidacloprid. Generally, azines belongs to class of organic molecules exhibiting the =N–N= linkage (Chourasiya et al., 2019). These organic compounds are generally formed by the reaction of hydrazine either with aldehydes and ketones (Chourasiya et al., 2019). The main advantage of using these compounds as QDs sensors are their ease of synthesis (Chourasiya et al., 2016), their ecofriendly by-products and degraded products and the amount of azines used in the sensing process (1 mg). The synthesized compounds were further processed into its nanostructures in order to establish a fluorescence approach for the imidacloprid recognition. The advantages of the synthesized sensor over enzymatic assays are their high thermal stability, ecofriendlyby-products and longer shelf life.

Laboratory grade pesticides, aminoguanidine hydrochloride, and 4-fluoro benzaldehyde were procured from Merck. The fluorescence studies were conducted by Shimadzu fluorescence spectrophotometer. The TEM and DLS studies were performed using JEOL TEM and Malvern Zeta Sizer respectively. All the studies were performed in double distilled water at room temperature (HiMedia Laboratories Pvt., India). The DFT studies were performed using Gaussian. The quantum chemical calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 suite of programs. Geometry optimization of compounds was performed by DFT using B3LYP method. The basis set used was 6-311+G (d,p). The frequency calculations were carried out on all the structures to verify character of stationary points (minima v/s saddle point). The particle size distribution was determined using the Imagej software.

To synthesise (3), 4-fluoro benzaldehyde (1) was treated with aminoguanidine hydrochloride (2) in water and the resulting mixture was agitated using magnetic stir until the precipitates get formed (Scheme 1). The obtained precipitates were filtered and dried to get the final compound. The sensing properties of the synthesized compound was explored by processing them into the organic nanoparticles (ONPs). For the preparation of ONPs, nano-precipitation method has been utilized. QDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method. The characterisation of the prepared nanostructures was done using TEM in order to find the size and morphology of prepared nanostructures.

The photophysical studies of the nanostructures were performed using fluorescence spectroscopy. The recognition studies were performed by screening the prepared nanostructures against various kinds of pesticides (organophosphate pesticides and neonicotinoid). The emission spectra of the prepared solution was recorded after 20 min. If the emission response shows change with any of the particular toxin then further studies such as titration studies, competitive studies, effect of environmental conditions and real time sample analysis were performed. The detection limit and quantification limit were calculated using the 3 sigma method. The interference studies were performed by analysing the developed sensor response with that specific toxin in presence of other similar competing toxins. The real sample analysis was also performed by spiking the sample with the known concentration of toxin and further recovery was calculated.

The compound 3 was synthesized by treatment of 4-fluoro benzaldehyde (1) with aminoguanidine hydrochloride (2) in water by adding 2 mL of NaOHaq. solution (1 N) and the mixture were stirred under ambient conditions for 45 min. The obtained mixture was filtered to get the crude precipitates and the precipitates were further washed (2–3 times) with unionised water to get rid of excess NaOH and the purified product was further oven dried at 50 °C to obtain 3. The formation of the product was initially confirmed by thin layer chromatography using MeOH/DCM (5:95). The synthesized compound was characterized using the 1H, 13C, 19F NMR and HRMS (See SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nanostructures (organic nanoparticles and quantum dots)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of ONPs basically involves the chemical interactions or self-assembly of different kinds of organic molecules via two methods viz top-down strategy and bottom-up strategy. The top-down process basically includes the processing of large molecules into tiny particles via mechanical milling or lithography whereas the bottom-up approach consists of the organization of tiny particles. Out of both these approaches, the bottom-up strategy was preferred over top-down technique has the limitation such as energy intensiveness, uncontrollable condition to get the desired size (Verma et al., 2020). Hence, in the current research, the ONPs of 3 were synthesized using bottom-up approach. Reprecipitation method was employed for the preparation which basically works on the solubility differences of organic molecules in two different phases. For this purpose, firstly, the organic compound was dissolved in an organic solvent which was further introduced in water which alters the microenvironment of the synthesized compound and is responsible for the formation of their nanoparticle. (Verma et al., 2020), (Verma et al., 2020) For carrying out the process, 1 mg of 3 was dissolved in DMSO (1 mL) which was further injected in 100 mL of double distilled water using a micro syringe under sonication conditions. After complete addition, the solution was sonicated for further 15 min to obtain the nanoparticles. Fig. 2 represents the schematic diagram for the synthesis of ONPs of compound 3.

The synthesized nanoparticles were further processed into their Quantum Dots (QDs) using the hydrothermal method. Among all the reported methods for the preparation of QDs, the hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) (Titirici and Antonietti, 2010), (Wang and Hu, 2014) or solvothermal carbonization is considered as the cheaper, ecofriendly and non-toxic pathway. Generally, the solution of the organic components like citric acid (Zhu et al., 2013), glucose (Yang et al., 2011), banana juice (De and Karak, 2013), chitosan (Yang et al., 2012), and protein (Zhang et al., 2012) were placed in a hydrothermal reactor at elevated temperature. Hence, the synthesized ONPs were placed in hydrothermal at 200 °C for the duration of 6 h. The preliminary confirmation of synthesis of QDs was done by observing a change in color from colourless to blue under U.V. light. The formation of the quantum dots was confirmed by TEM.

The fluorescence spectroscopic studies were conducted in order to confirm the formation of ONPs and QDs where the emission maxima corresponding to ONPs and QDs was found to be 345 nm and 400 nm respectively. The emission maxima for QDs is attributed towards the blue emission. Fig. 3 shows the overlapping fluorescence spectra of ONPs and QDs.

The synthesized ONPs and QDs were characterized using the TEM studies. The TEM images represented the spherical shape ONPs of size varying from 20 to 60 nm (Fig. 4 a and c). The TEM analysis of QDs depicted monodispersed and spherical morphology with the average size varying between 6 and 20 nm respectively (Fig. 4b and d). Further, the band gap (Eg = hc/λ; where λ is the emission wavelength maxima) of the synthesized ONPs and QDs was calculated using fluorescence studies which was estimated to be 3.59 eV and 3.03 eV for ONPs and QDs respectively. The increase in the band gap for QDs is attributed towards the quantum confinement effect and hence shows the changes in the optical profile of QDs as compared to that of ONPs. Further, the average particle size was measured using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and was estimated to be 37.1 nm and 13.56 nm for ONPs and QDs, respectively (See SI; Table S1 and S2).

The synthesized ONPs were further investigated for their recognition potential against the pesticides such as chlorpyrifos, glycophosphate, phosmet, ethion, clothianidin, acetamipirid and thiacloprid using fluorescence studies at an excitation wavelength of 278 nm. No selectivity was observed for the screened pesticides (Fig. 5 ).

In order to enhance their fluorescence emission properties, the synthesized ONPs were proceesed into their quantum dots hydrothermally which will tune the photophysical characteristics. The prepared quantum dots were also screened against the various kinds of pesticides such as chlorpyrifos, glycophosphate, phosmet, ethion, clothianidin, acetamipirid and thiacloprid to evaluate their detection efficacy towards these pesticides. The quantum dots exhibit emission at a wavelength of 400 nm. All the other pesticides exhibit an increment in the fluorescence response of the QDs solution except imidacloprid. The introduction of imidacloprid leads to the diminish in the fluorescence of QDs. After getting the variation in the fluorescence response of QDs with imidacloprid, the QDs were screened against various kinds of metals ions such as Na+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Ba2+ and no variation has been observed in the fluorescence spectra of QDs which leads to the selectivity of QDs towards the detection of imidacloprid (Fig. 5b). Further to check the sensitivity and specificity of the synthesized sensor, titration studies were performed by adding the subsequent decreasing concentration of imidacloprid pesticide (1 μM–100 μM) to the QDs solution (Fig. 6 a). A normalised decrement was observed on increasing the concentration of the imidacloprid to the QDs solution (Fig. 6b).

By considering the fluorescence emission at 400 nm, a linear curve (Stern Volmer Plot) has been plotted by taking the fluorescene intensity maxima value (Fig. 6c) which shows the normalised response in the decrement of fluorescence of QDs with rise in imidacloprid concentration (1 μM-100 μM). The linear curve data was utilized to plot the Stern volmer graph and to find the quenching constant using the stern volmer equation which is
F o
F i
= 1 + K s v
[ Q ]

Where Fo and Fi are the fluorescence intensities of pure QDs and QDs with varied concentrations of imidacloprid, [Q] and Ksv are quencher concentration and stern volmer coefficient, respectively. Further, the LOD and LOQ were estimated using 3 sigma method which was estimated to be 4.53 nM and 13.72 nM respectively. For calculating the stern volmer constant, the stern volmer equation was used. The Stern Volmer plot was plotted by taking Fo/Fi as y-axis and concentration of quencher [Q] as x-axis (Fig. 6c). The calculated stern volmer constant was calculated to be 1.21 × 102 M-1.

Moreover, to confirm the selectivity of the synthesized nanostructures, interference studies were conducted by adding the potential competing pesticides such as chlorpyrifos, glycophosphate, phosmet, ethion, azinphos-methyl and collectively in presence of all the pesticides. No considerable change was observed in the fluorescence spectra of the ONPs + imidacloprid solution (Fig. 6d). In addition to pesticides, the interference studies were carried out against various kinds of metal ions such as Na+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Ba2+ and no significant change was observed in the fluorescence behaviour of the QDs with imidacloprid (Fig. 7 a). After analysing the selectivity and sensitivity of the synthesized sensor, stability of the fluorescent probe was examined by varying the environmental factors such as pH and temperature. The synthesized sensor was found to be stable over a temperature varies from 15 to 55 °C and over a pH of 2–10 (Fig. 7b and c).

After carrying out all the studies, the relative quantum yield was also calculated using fluorescein as the reference material. The relative quantum yield was calculated using the below equation which is
φ A
=
φ s
*
(
F A
/
F s
)
*
( O . D
. s
/ O . D
. A
)

Where, φA and φs are the quantum yield of the sample and standard; FA and Fs are the integrated fluorescence intensity of the sample and the standard; and O.D.s and O.D.A are the absorbance of the standard and the sample, respectively (Würth et al., 2013). Using the above equation, the calculated fluorescence quantum yield of the QDs was found to be 0.18 or 18%.

The ONPs and QDs were screened for various pesticides. The ONPs does not show any selective response for the screened pesticides, and the possible reason may be the strong interaction of ONPs with water molecules through hydrogen bonding and thus ONPs were not able to interact with the pesticides. However, in case of QDs the selective fluorescence quenching was observed with imidacloprid which may be attributed towards the strong interactions of imidacloprid with QDs (Fig. 8). Various factors assist in this which are a) high surface area, b) at high temperature the hydrogen bonding with the organic molecules get weaker and thus provide an opportunity for the interaction with imidacloprid, and c) Quantum confinement effect. Further, the interaction of imidacloprid and how it interacts was investigated using computational studies.

The experimental work suggests that there is a complex formation between the organic quantum dots (3) and the imidacloprid (pesticide) which is responsible for the observed fluorescence (400 nm). The hydrogen bonding interactions are primarily responsible for such complex formations. Quantum chemical calculations were performed to explore the thermodynamic feasibility and 3D structures of the most possible complex responsible for the fluorescence. To explore the possible 3D structure of complex which is responsible for the observed fluorescence, quantum chemical calculations were carried out using Gaussian 09 package. Geometry optimizations were performed by density functional theory (DFT) B3LYP/6-311+G (d,p) level. (Frisch et al., 2016)., (Parr, 1989)

For this study, four different possibilities each with only associative forces (H-bonds) have been considered. They are labelled as Com-a, Com-b and Com-c carry H-bond interactions between one unit of azine and one unit of imidacloprid while as, Com-d contains two units of azine hydrogen bonded to imidacloprid (Fig. 9 a). The 3D structures reveal that guanidine portion of the azine is involved in H-bonding interactions with the imidacloprid (I). The energy required for the formation of these four complexes are 3.00, 2.21, 2.67 and 5.65 kcal/mol respectively (Table 1), all are marginally endergonic except Com-d. To estimate the excitation energies and the λ max values, the TD-DFT calculations were performed. The estimated λ max values have been compared with experimental results. The experimental λ max values of imidacloprid (260 nm) have been compared with that of the calculated value (245 nm). The λ max values were found significantly comparable, hence, building confidence in the ability of quantum chemical methods in estimating the λ max values. Now, the experimentally estimated value for the complex (270 nm, Fig. S5) is compared with the calculated values of these four complexes (Table 2 ). This comparative analysis helped in identifying Com-b as the most possible structure of the Imidacloprid-Azine complex. Fig. 9 depicts the 3D-representation of all the four complexes. The HOMO-LUMO energy values and their differences along with the contour maps of the molecular orbitals are provided in Fig. 10 . Many other alternative 3D-structures were also considered, but ignored because of very high ΔG values as well as less comparable λ max values. Generally, it is known that any compound exhibits fluorescence when it enters into rigid molecular environment and this increase in rigidity changes the observed fluorescence. The observed rigidity is very clear in all the complexes.

The formation of Com-b was confirmed by FTIR. For comparison studies, the FTIR of spectrum of 3, 3+imidacloprid and imidacloprid (reference) were considered (Graily Moradi et al., 2019). The 3 shows transmittance at 3432, 3317 and 1638 cm⁠−1 that belongs to the N–H stretching (3432, 3317 cm−1) and CN respectively. The FTIR spectrum of the complex (3 + imidacloprid) exhibit transmittance at 3548 cm−1 which is complementary to the hydrogen bonding (NO2- -H-N) between the 3 and the imidacloprid (Fig. 11 a). The spike at 3211 cm−1 is due to stretching of N–H (N–H⋯N) and the shift in the stretching frequency was observed owing to the weakening of N–H bond as it participates in H-bonding. The peak of sensor-3 at 1638 corresponds to the imine, however, the peak intensity was diminished which confirms the participation of iminic nitrogen (-CN) with the imidacloprid via intermolecular hydrogen bonding (N–H⋯N). The new peaks in the reason of 850–600 cm−1 arises due to the C–Cl stretching. The FTIR analysis supports the formation of Com-b as well as Com-d, however, among these two considered complexes, Com-b is energetically favourable (Fig. 11b).

The practical applications of the synthesized QDs based fluorescence method were tested by quantifying the spiked concentration of imidacloprid in water and fruit juice (Orange juice) samples. For the quantification, 25, 50, 70, 90 nM of imidacloprid was spiked separately in the aforementioned samples and the concentration was calculated using the above said protocol. The concentration was found to be the 23.6 nM, 48.3 nM, 68.7 nM and 86.4 nM in water samples and 22.5 nM, 47.6 nM, 66.3 nM and 84.2 nM in juice sample. The recovery varies from 96% to 98% and 90%–94% for water and juice samples respectively. Hence, the reported fluorescence-based detection technique is found to be encouraging analytical approach to recognize imidacloprid in aqueous and orange juice sample.

In the present work, the non-fluorescent (E)-(4-fluorophenyl)-1,1-diamino-2,3-diazabuta-1,3-diene (3) was synthesized using green approach. From the organic molecule, the non-fluorescent organic nanoparticles of 3 was prepared using re-precipitation method. Further, the ONPs were transformed into the blue fluorescent Quantum Dots using hydrothermal method. The prepared QDs were investigated as a luminescent approach for the recognition of imidacloprid selectively. The selective response of the probe towards the pesticide was attributed due to the interaction of functional attachment including nitro (-NO2) and amino (–NH–) present in imidacloprid with the functional groups such as amine (-NH2) and imine (-CN-) of QDs. These interactions are highly responsible for the considerable quenching of the emission intensity of fluorescent probe. The LOD for imidacloprid was found to be 4.53 nM. The synthesized probe exhibits a potential efficacy for the quantitation of above said specific pesticide in water and orange juice sample at a very low concentration i.e., 25 nM. The developed method is enzyme free, easy to conduct and possess high sensitivity and selectivity towards imidacloprid. Moreover, the QDs synthesized are prepared by environment friendly approach.

Monika Bhattu: Executions of idea, Meenakshi Verma: Revise it critically, Aabid A. Wani: Performed the computational study Prasad V. Bharatam: Supervision on the computational details, Shweta Sareen: Revise it critically, Deepika Kathuria: Generation of idea, analysis and revised it critically.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: P.V.B. and A.A.W. thanks financial support from Department of Biotechnology, New-Delhi (Grant no.: BT/PR40164/BTIS/137/17/2021), India for providing a funding.
